speaker: interviewer
text: You can begin.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two, Renate Rossmere is being interviewed. You kept that little bit of medication in your pocket book for your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, except that it ran out.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was so nervous.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were all very nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took it too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I just had it in my pocket book. And when I looked, it had run out. So I could never give him the medication that he was supposed to have. But he did-- he was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a general feeling among you and your friends that this-- this preparation for leaving, this nervousness, was so prevalent among all of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a lot of nervousness amongst the friends. And some friends, at every knock on their door, they didn't know whether this was not a Nazi coming to take away their loved ones, their father, their brother. And there was no rest. there. Was-- it was everybody was nervous and terribly frightened of what might happen to them, especially after the [INAUDIBLE] in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you'd see your father again?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH] I didn't, no. He had always said-- he didn't think that they could do anything to him because he had been in the First World War. And he had received the Iron Cross, and that they wouldn't touch somebody like that. Of course, that turned out to be not true. So no, though there was anxiety, terrible anxiety in all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you pack in your suitcase?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Just the bare necessities, as far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any little trinket or something that-- that you wanted to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really am not sure. I-- I do know I took a little watch which my mother's best friend had given, which you hang around your neck. And I took that with me. That meant a lot to me. Apart from that, it was very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other children whom you knew on the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, except yes, there was one. And that was my husband to be. Our mothers were friends. And we had dancing lessons together as was done at that point, shortly before we left. Now, my husband was very experienced in dancing. He had taken this course before. And so when his mother said, please dance with Renate Loeser, he was not too pleased. And when my mother said, be nice to Andrew Rossmere, I was not too pleased. And indeed, throughout the course-- which wasn't finished, because we had to stop because of all things going on outside-- we danced once. I think he asked me to dance once, and never again. And I thought he was terribly conceited.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, our mothers took us to the Hauptbahnhof, Hamburg Hauptbahnhof, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the train. And the train, it was all curtains. All the windows were closed, and the curtains were drawn so that no one would, on the outside, would see Jewish children. Could do them some harm. So we couldn't-- there, the two mothers stood, our two mothers, and we couldn't wave to them or say goodbyes through the window to them because it wasn't allowed to draw-- undraw the curtains. So that was very hard to see them standing there, and you know, through the-- when you went in through the door. And look back, and there they were. They were trying very hard not to cry, trying to be very brave. Did you ever expect to see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really didn't know. We didn't know. We were hoping that we would, but you just didn't know. It was a very terrible feeling, that not knowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were many of these other children going? Did they all have specific homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most of them didn't. Most of them ended up in camps. I-- my brother and I were very lucky to have a home to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know your aunt before this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did know her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did know her very well. She had lived in Hamburg many, many years. She had emigrated in 1935 to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a trip was it on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the train left in the morning, and we went via Holland. I remember that, and stopped in Holland. And we were greeted by people from the Red Cross. And they gave us some food and something to drink. And then we boarded the boat. And I think it was the next day that we arrived in England by boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you separated from your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were together the entire time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you need to reassure him? Did you then become the-- the mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, yes. Yes, it was harder for him, I think, being separated from our parents than for me at that point. Because he was so little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your aunt there waiting for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure about that. I've thought about that many times. I think we met her later. But on the boat, there was our doctor, our family doctor, who was involved in the Jewish organization, he and his wife. And he was there. Why, I shall-- I do not know. But that was a good moment when we saw him in England, coming onto the boat to greet us. And then I think after that my aunt met us at the train, but that is a very vague memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What then is the most outstanding memory of that separation of all the children? Of course, you and your brother specifically, but what-- what do you-- what do you see? What do you see in your mind happening when all these children are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've all-- I was very shy and kept very much to myself, and didn't talk very much to anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Basically, I found people were cheerful. We were singing. At least on the outside, nobody was really showing what they felt inside on this train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People talked to each other. There were games played.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was an amazing atmosphere, really, because everybody must have felt very lost and uncertain of the future. And would they ever see their parents again? And many, many of them, of course, never did. So there was an air of lightheartedness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the-- on the train, which didn't seem real, and that everybody was putting on a good face to a bad situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet Dutch people when you arrived, or were you just whisked right on through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that-- that was just a stop, and then-- I'm sure there were Dutch people who were serving us and greeted us. But we didn't have anything, any other communication with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How fluent were you in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly fluent, I'd had English in school for six years, I think. So, of course, you never learn it until you actually live in that country, and live with an English family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother, was he fluent at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, no. No, he hadn't had English yet in his class. He was too little.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult for him. But of course, we joined a household where German was spoken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like, when your aunt picked you up? Your aunt was married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was going through a divorce at that point, unfortunately. She had emigrated to England with her two children, and in order to join her husband. And on arrival in-- in England, she was told that he had found somebody else. And that they needed to get a divorce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was going through this while she was at the same time attempting to get the family out. And she lived in a small house in Golders Green and with her two daughters. And-- Were they older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was the oldest of all my cousins. And one cousin, Lee, was three years younger, and Naomi was younger than my brother. So she was-- don't remember-- maybe five, six. And my aunt was lovely and very caring. But she went to work. So somebody had to look after the household. So that's what I chose to do. And of course, the children were all sent off to school. And I looked after the household. And then, luckily, a few months later in April or May, she was able to bring my parents over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how were you able to be in touch with your parents during this time, with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes. Yes, we did write. Yes, we were, not very much, but we did have contact. And I think my father was released-- I'm not quite sure when-- whether it was January 19, 1939, or February. I'm not quite sure how soon. But once he-- they had the documentation that there was a home in England that somebody would vouch for them, they let him go. Thank god, and the same as my uncle too. And was Mr. Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was the first to come over, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my parents, yes. And so then, of course, we needed to get a new house, a larger house. And indeed, they found a very nice one at 1 Corringway, also in Golders Green. And I remember the day my parents arrived, which was terribly exciting. And they arrived by taxi with all their suitcases. And I remember it being very exciting and greeting them and-- at the taxi. And then, closing the door and shutting the door so violently that the glass of the taxi door fell out. [LAUGHS] It was just a very, very emotional moment to see them again, see my father again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father look like after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of the hair had grown back. But he still looked very thin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you notice any--

speaker: interviewee
text: And haggard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And haggard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you could see a difference in him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but of course, he was, you know, very lucky. He was only in the camp considerably small time compared to many, many, many other people. It's only a few months, but it was bad enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to meet other children during the time that you were keeping house for your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, one or two, yes. My friend from-- [? Steffi ?] from Hamburg came over. She also came over. And then eventually, my grandparents came over, and an uncle, and other relatives. All were brought up by my aunt who saved all our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both sets of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the-- no, my paternal grandparents had died previously of normal, natural death. And my maternal grandparents came out, came to England. And we all lived in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your aunt move too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, of course, she was the main person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many of you lived in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were 10 altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh. Was your father able to find work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you weren't allowed to work as a refugee in England at that point, only as a domestic. So that was very hard for him, not to be able to do his work which he loved. And it was, of course, very little money. And all our household was packed in crates and was supposed to be shipped to London. And was at the harbor, the port, in Hamburg, and at the outbreak of the war, it was totally bombed. And we never received anything. So then my parents really had nothing. They weren't allowed to take out much money. And all their household stuff burnt up. So there was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they really just packed clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their clothes, their immediate needs, and that was it. That-- none, none of the other things, the furniture, the bedding, the, you know, linens, all that-- memorabilia of our youth, of our background, that was all burnt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother able to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he went to school, and he learned English very quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was at the end of your education.

speaker: interviewee
text: More of less-- I went to school very briefly in England--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your formal education.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in London to learn a little bit of English. But basically, I was needed at home. I needed to-- to run the household for 10 people, and to do the cooking. And they seemed to like my cooking. So-- [LAUGHS] And my mother went to do the marketing for the family, and my aunt went to work. And my father did the chores around the house. And the children were in school. And I was running the rest of the household for six years. I consider that my war work. And I was glad I was able to do this for my family. During that time, I read a great deal, one book after the other. And somehow, got some kind of education that way. That, of course, was a very haphazard way to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was happening around you in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGH] let's see. Well, the war started in September. And with that, the bombing started as far as I remember. And I was at my sixteenth birthday in March. I was considered dangerous because I had not been in front of a tribunal. See, there were tribunals for all the refugees in order to determine whether they were dangerous, half dangerous, or not at all dangerous-- ABC. If you were a C, you were OK. Since I was-- had just turned 16, there had been no time to put me in front of a tribunal. And consequently, one day, there was a police at the door. And he said to my mother that he's come to take me away because they weren't sure whether I was dangerous or not. And so that was quite a shock for my mother. We quickly had to pack a tiny suitcase. And I remember packing a nail file, and he watched while we were packing this suitcase. And he said, no nail files, no scissors. And I was taken to the local police station. And I remember having to go to the bathroom. And a police woman came with me. And it was impossible to go, [LAUGHS] because I was just so uptight having-- being-- ugh, having somebody come with me wherever I went. Anyway, from there we were shipped to Liverpool into an enormous hall. I think it was a sports arena. And there were hundreds and hundreds of refugees all in this arena. And I remember sitting next to a woman. And I looked at her, and I didn't like her looks. And I was afraid that if I would stick with this woman, I would be shipped and housed together with her wherever they were going to take us. And so I moved away under some pretext. And I went around the arena and looked for somebody who looked, to my mind, pleasant. And I found a very lovely lady. And indeed, we were housed together. And she was a wonderful person all through the internment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the other people in this huge arena?

speaker: interviewee
text: All refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: All from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- well, possibly from other. May have been from Austria too, probably were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: But mainly-- any age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any age. No, just women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I only remember women. The men were-- were gathered separately, and sent to separate. We were all sent to the Isle of Man. But the men to a different part than the women. And the women were then housed. And this was a holiday, a vacation place, the Isle of Man, with lovely beaches, with huge hotels and guest houses. And a lot of the people were housed in the guest houses and the hotels, of course. I was lucky to be in a guest house, a much smaller place [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with some maybe 10 other people, young people and older people and this lovely lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to think. I don't remember her first name. But I think it was Lowenthal. I've always was hoping I would somehow run into her again. I never did. And there we were on the Isle of Man, not able to go anywhere. At first, we were allowed to use the beaches, and later on, not. It was much more restricted later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read, went for walks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in communication with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, very little, it was very difficult to communicate with your family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any bad--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was censored. And you know, and they were just-- the British were very afraid of spies. And that Hitler was-- would come over, and take the country. And they did-- this was a precautionary measure, which we understood. But it still was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak then?

speaker: interviewee
text: English and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the-- to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the others, I think it was, yes, a mixture.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a mixture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any effort to organize yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schools were-- a school was organized, yes. And later on, we could take jobs. I briefly took on the job of delivering the mail, because I thought it would be so wonderful for these people waiting so eagerly to hear from their loved ones. And I was very disappointed in the loneliness of this job. You walked around the island [LAUGHS] of the town, and all by yourself. And delivering mail, people didn't hardly greeted you. And it was different than I'd imagined. Anyway, that was for just a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any mistreatment of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no mistreatment at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but cramped quarters. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that I know of. There-- there may have been in this town. Not in the house, per se, where we lived. I had to share a bed with a-- with another teenager. And she always took most of the blanket, which I resented. And she was released before me. The one thing I remember, we had rice pudding every day. To this day, it's hard for me to eat rice pudding. [LAUGHS] But everybody was very kind. And of course, the worst thing, I think, of that experience were the rumors. The rumors that London had burned down, that people had died, that possibly your family was hit by a bomb. That kind of thing was very hard to [INTERPOSING VOICES].

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was more of the fear of what was happening to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the rumors you hear, which you'd-- you believe, but you didn't really know whether they were true. It was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there services, religious services held there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not as far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: If there were, I did not attend them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you questioned by-- by any authorities as to your patriotism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. No, we were really-- it was being herded into one place, and kept their isolated from the rest, just in case we could be dangerous. And we were together with spies. There were people there who were known spies. We knew of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, they were in the hotels, so we had nothing to do with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is something that was talked about a lot, that there were people who really belonged there. But a lot of people didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one day when we were allowed to meet the men-- relatives, friends, and so on. But it was so badly organized that when I arrived, wherever we were supposed to meet them, my father was not there. He was interned also. And I never met him there. And I was released after about four months, due to my mother having said that she was not well, and she needed me at home. I was very much needed there. And that was one reason-- one way I could get released. That was a nice-- nice feeling to be able to come home and not have one's freedom curbed. It was really the first time that I had felt what it's like not to be able to go do what you want to do. But under really very mild circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were now 17?

speaker: interviewer
text: 16-- what were your thoughts of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of-- you had so much time on your hands. What kinds of thoughts--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bless you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. What kinds of thoughts occupied your mind? Were you-- was it worry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wanting to be free, worried about the family, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes, a lot of worries-- worried about the rumors that you heard, worried about what would happen to you, oneself. Uncertainty of the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had your dreams been for your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, dreams were very scarce at that time because everything was so uncertain. So I think my dreams had been to get married one day to a nice young man, and that's about it. Have a career, but I wasn't sure what kind of career. And I knew I wanted to work with children. And that wish was fulfilled when I was-- at 18. 20, sorry-- at 20, I could leave the house. Leave the household, finally, and start a career, and be trained as a nursery-- nurse's teacher, a nursery teacher. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] it was a place where you stayed overnight. It was a residential place. And during the day, the children came. All, of course, from wartime mothers, who brought the children at 7:00 in the morning and picked them up at 7:00 at night because they did all work in factory work. And so it was a very good training. You have children from a few weeks old to five years, through five. And you know, so you got trained each stage of their lives, which was very wonderful. And you went to school at the same time.
